C-DE211 · Data Analysis · EUI Spring 2026
Egypt Food Crisis: Currency Devaluation,
Market Shocks & Food Security (2010–2024)
Datasets: WFP Food Prices (16,000+ records) · WFP Market Metadata · FAO Food Security Indicators  |  Tools: Python · Pandas · SciPy · Scikit-learn
WFP Data FAO Data 3 Hypotheses Food Security Egypt 2010–2024 Exchange Rate
9.4%
Undernourishment 2024
43%
Cereal Import Dep.
0.9962
Regression R²
<0.05
H1 p-value (both)
Abstract
Egypt imports over 55% of its wheat, exposing 105 million people to global commodity shocks amplified by a weakening currency. Two EGP devaluations — 50% in Nov 2016 (8.8→18/USD) and a further collapse in 2022 (>30/USD) — provide natural experiments. Using WFP retail price records and FAO welfare indicators, we find: both devaluations caused statistically significant price increases across all commodities (Mann-Whitney U, p<0.05); Cairo prices exceed the national average for all 12 staple commodities tested; no significant difference exists in inflation rates between staple and non-staple categories (ANOVA p=0.31), meaning macro FX drivers dominate over sectoral dynamics. A linear regression model achieves R²=0.9962, consistent with inter-commodity Spearman correlations above 0.90. Egypt's undernourishment rate nearly doubled since 2019, reaching 9.4% in 2024.
Introduction & Methods
Why this study?

When the EGP loses value, every imported calorie gets more expensive within weeks. Egypt imports more wheat than almost any other country and has historically held down retail prices through a large bread subsidy — but the subsidy cannot fully absorb a 50% exchange rate shock.

This paper documents the 2016 and 2022 transmission mechanism and tests whether devaluations affect staple and non-staple foods differently. The answer has direct budget and welfare implications.

Datasets
WFP Food Prices
Monthly retail prices, 30+ commodities, all Egypt governorates, Jan 2010–mid 2024. 16,234 obs after cleaning.
WFP Market Metadata
Governorate, coordinates, market type for each monitored market. Enables Cairo vs. national comparison.
FAO Food Security Indicators
Annual series 2002–2024: PoU, PMSFI, PSFI, Cereal Import Dependency Ratio (CIDR).
Analysis Pipeline
1
EDA — distributions, boxplots, heatmaps
2
Cleaning — standardize, parse dates, forward-fill ≤3-month gaps
3
Feature engineering — MoM inflation, pre/post flags, staple classification
4
Hypothesis testing — Mann-Whitney U, violin comparison, ANOVA
5
Spearman correlation matrix across all commodity pairs
6
Linear regression — R² evaluation + residual inspection
Hypotheses
H1 — Devaluation & Prices
Both devaluations caused statistically significant retail food price increases for staple and non-staple commodities.
H2 — Urban Premium
Cairo staple prices consistently exceed the national market average across all 12 tested commodities.
H3 — Category Inflation
Staple foods inflate at a higher monthly rate than non-staple foods during 2020–2024.
Results Summary
TestStatResult
H1 · 2016p=0.000416✓ SUPPORTED
H1 · 2022p=0.008212✓ SUPPORTED
H2 · MarketCairo > all 12✓ SUPPORTED
H3 · ANOVAp=0.3094✗ REJECTED
RegressionR²=0.9962✓ EXCELLENT
Food Security & Price Trends
Fig 1 — FAO Food Security Indicators (2002–2024)
Fig 1 · FAO Food Security Indicators for Egypt
Undernourishment (top-left) nearly doubled from 4.9% (2010) to 9.4% (2024). Cereal import dependency (bottom-right) peaked at ~51% in 2019–20. Red dashed = 2016 devaluation; grey dotted = 2022.
↳ Reading the chart
The 4-panel layout lets you track short-run vulnerability (food insecurity %) and structural exposure (import dependency) simultaneously. The 1–2 year lag between the devaluation markers and the welfare inflection points is visible — exchange rate shocks reach undernourishment statistics after households exhaust savings and coping strategies.
⊙ Why FAO indicators?
FAO constructs these with a consistent model-based methodology back to 2002, enabling cross-decade comparison. Household surveys (HIES) would need restricted microdata and suffer from methodological breaks between rounds. CIDR is chosen specifically because Egypt's vulnerability is structural (import exposure), not a production failure.
Fig 2 — Commodity Prices 2010–2024
Fig 2 · Egypt Food Commodity Prices — The Devaluation Shocks
Sunflower oil: 5→23 EGP post-2016, then →41 EGP post-2022. Sugar peaked ~45 EGP. Wheat flour held at ~7 EGP by subsidy until 2022, then rose to ~13 EGP. Prices were flat for 6 years — then lurched twice.
⊙ Why line chart?
14 years of monthly prices across 6 commodities demands continuity — bars hide the trend; scatter hides it further. The near-vertical steps at both devaluation lines are the primary visual evidence of rapid FX pass-through. Vertical markers (not shaded bands) are used to avoid obscuring prices during the shock itself.
Fig 3 — Annual Price Distributions
Fig 3 · Price Distribution by Year (2010–2024)
Median rose from ~8 EGP (2010) to ~27 EGP (2024). The IQR widened dramatically in 2022–24 as freely-traded commodities diverged from subsidized ones — visible as expanding boxes and outliers.
⊙ Why box plots?
15 years × full distribution = 15 histograms if done naively. Box plots compress each year into a single element (median, IQR, whiskers, outliers), making year-over-year evolution readable in one panel. The non-parametric summary is appropriate given that food prices are right-skewed — a mean ± SD summary would be misleading.
Hypothesis Results H1 & H2
Fig 4 — H1: Price Distributions Before vs After Devaluation
Fig 4 · H1 — Devaluation Impact (left: 2016 p=0.000416 · right: 2022 p=0.008212)
Blue = before, red = after each devaluation. Both staple and non-staple categories shifted upward significantly. The 2022 panel shows larger absolute jumps and more extreme outliers — the Russia-Ukraine shock compounded the FX effect.
✓ H1 SUPPORTED — both devaluations, p < 0.05
↳ Reading the chart
The median staple price rose ~30% post-2016 and ~60% post-2022. Non-staple price shifts were larger in absolute EGP terms (wider IQR) but equally significant statistically — anticipating the H3 finding that inflation pressure is uniform across categories.
⊙ Why Mann-Whitney U (not t-test)?
Shapiro-Wilk confirmed non-normality in all price distributions (p<0.001). The t-test requires normality; with fat-tailed, right-skewed commodity prices and ~300 obs per window, CLT normalisation is not guaranteed. Mann-Whitney U is rank-based with no distributional assumptions — it tests whether one distribution is stochastically larger than another, which is precisely the right question for devaluation analysis. Welch's t-test was run as a check; it agreed (p<0.01), but we report the non-parametric result.
Fig 5 — H2: Cairo vs National Average
Fig 5 · H2 — Staple Price Distribution: Cairo (green) vs National Average (orange)
Cairo exceeds national average for all 12 staple commodities. Oil (cotton) shows the widest gap (~87 EGP Cairo peak vs ~25 EGP national). Wheat flour and potatoes approach parity — subsidy pass-through is working for those specific goods.
✓ H2 SUPPORTED — Cairo > national for all 12 commodities
↳ Reading the chart
The premium is not uniform. Freely traded goods (oils, lentils) show 2–3× Cairo markups. Subsidized goods (wheat flour, potatoes) near-equalize. This means the subsidy system is working as designed for targeted products — but urban households bear a larger burden on everything else.
⊙ Why violin plots (not bar/t-test)?
Several commodities have bimodal price distributions (pre- and post-devaluation price levels). A bar chart of means would place the average between two modes — a price that almost never actually occurred. Violin plots show the full empirical density, revealing the structural price shifts without any parametric assumptions. A t-test of means would be technically valid but less informative about the shape of the access gap.
H3 · Correlations · Model · Conclusions
Fig 6 — H3: Inflation by Category & MoM Distribution
Fig 6a · H3 Inflation by Category
Staple mean 2.41% vs Non-Staple 1.77%. ANOVA p=0.3094 — not significant.
Fig 6b · MoM Inflation Distribution
Centered near 0%, right-skewed — inflation occurs in bursts, not continuously.
✗ H3 NOT SUPPORTED — p = 0.3094
↳ What this means
Food inflation in Egypt is not a "staple problem" — it's systemic. The exchange rate shock hits sunflower oil and wheat simultaneously. Commodity-specific price caps provide uneven protection; the whole food basket inflates together.
⊙ Why ANOVA (not Mann-Whitney U here)?
H3 compares mean monthly inflation rates — a derived variable that is less skewed than raw prices and approaches normality by CLT, justifying a parametric mean comparison. Unlike H1 (full distribution comparison), the research question here is specifically about whether the average rate differs between groups. Letter-value (boxen) plots reveal tail behavior beyond what standard box plots show — critical for understanding extreme price episodes.
Fig 7 — Inter-Commodity Correlations
Fig 7 · Spearman Correlation Heatmap — 30 Commodities
Most pairs: r > 0.90. Garlic and artificial ghee are outliers with weaker correlations, suggesting locally determined or domestically produced pricing independent of the FX shock.
⊙ Why Spearman (not Pearson)?
Pearson assumes normality and linear relationships — both violated by right-skewed commodity prices, especially around shock events where prices jump non-linearly. Spearman's rank-based coefficient captures monotonic (not just linear) co-movement, which is the appropriate measure for "do these prices move together?" The near-universal r > 0.90 confirms a single dominant latent driver: the exchange rate.
Fig 8 — Linear Regression Model (R² = 0.9962)
Fig 8 · Actual vs Predicted Prices & Residual Plot
Near-perfect diagonal alignment. Residuals centered at zero; slight heteroscedasticity at >80 EGP confirms that premium, freely-traded goods became more volatile post-2022.
⊙ Why linear regression (not tree/neural)?
The goal is explanation, not black-box prediction. Linear coefficients directly encode each feature's price contribution — the information needed for policy. With r > 0.90 across all pairs, the data is near-linearly separable: a random forest would add no predictive gain while destroying interpretability. The heteroscedasticity at high prices suggests a log-price extension as future work.
Conclusions & Policy Implications

Currency devaluation is the dominant proximate driver of food price inflation in Egypt. The macro channel — FX → import costs → retail prices — operates across all food categories simultaneously, as the H3 rejection confirms. This rules out commodity-specific interventions as the primary policy tool.

①Strategic FX reserves and standby IMF credit lines specifically earmarked for food import financing, to reduce peak exchange rate overshoot during devaluation events.
②Wheat import diversification away from Russia/Ukraine — geographic concentration amplified the 2022 shock dramatically.
③Expand cash transfer coverage (Egypt Food Subsidy Card) and index transfer amounts to the actual food price basket monthly — broader protection than commodity-specific subsidies at lower fiscal cost.
④Build an early-warning system monitoring 5–6 representative commodities monthly. With r > 0.90 across the basket, a small sentinel set proxies the full market reliably.
